name: Spain
summary: Spain offers a real software/AI shortage, cheap public MSc, a Dhaka-decided visa and the 2025 Reglamento (RD 1155/2024) giving students 30h/week work plus an EU Blue Card route from ~EUR 31k - but it is decisively weakened for this couple by a study-phase spouse work ban, study years counting only 50% toward PR, a 10-year citizenship clock forcing Bangladeshis to renounce (no dual), and a Madrid/Barcelona housing squeeze.

links.title: La Moncloa: new Immigration Regulation results (official, 21.11.2025)
Spain Visa Application - BLS International Bangladesh (official)

job-market.summary: 30k-50k ICT shortage, AI Engineer the most in-demand role 2026; Madrid/Barcelona hubs, EU Blue Card from ~EUR 31k.
job-market.reasoning: Spain is short 30,000-50,000 ICT specialists and AI Engineer is projected the single most in-demand role in 2026, with ~30% of AI/ML positions unfilled - ideal for two CS engineers. Madrid and Barcelona are top EU startup hubs that hire English-speaking international talent, and the EU Blue Card (2026 threshold EUR 39,269.92, reduced EUR 31,415.94 for shortage roles/recent graduates) gives a fast, labour-market-test-free sponsorship route for both spouses. Salaries trail Germany/Nordics, keeping it strong rather than world-leading.
evidence: Shortage of 30,000-50,000 ICT specialists; ICT workforce grew 6.8% YoY (2025-26)
AI Engineer projected most in-demand role in Spain 2026; ~30% of AI/ML roles unfilled
EU Blue Card 2026 threshold EUR 39,269.92; reduced EUR 31,415.94 for shortage/recent grads
Blue Card in-country switch, no labour-market test, ~20 day processing
Madrid/Barcelona top EU startup hubs; senior TC EUR 100k+ at international firms

links.title: EU Blue Card Spain 2026 - thresholds & processing (Jobbatical)
Most in-demand tech job in Spain 2026 (TechMag)

visa-access.summary: National student visa decided at Embassy in Dhaka (no New Delhi), modest ~EUR 600/month funds; but BD Schengen refusal 54.9% (2024).
visa-access.reasoning: Long-term/national student visas are submitted and decided directly at the Embassy of Spain in Dhaka (appointments by phone, no New Delhi travel), with modest financial proof of ~100% IPREM (EUR 600/month) and no interview - a mid-tier, workable process. The headline risk is the broader Bangladesh Schengen refusal rate, which jumped to 54.9% in 2024 (from 43.3%), though that figure covers short-stay visas; national study visas are a separate, document-driven track decided in Dhaka. Friction is real: all documents must be translated into Spanish and notarised, and appointment slots can be tight.
evidence: National/student visas decided at Embassy of Spain in Dhaka; no New Delhi routing
Proof of funds 100% IPREM = EUR 600/month for the student (2025-26)
Appointment by phone (+8801609208089, 14:00-15:30); Schengen via BLS Banani
Bangladesh overall Schengen refusal rate 54.9% in 2024 (up from 43.3% in 2023)
All documents must be translated to Spanish and notarised; biometrics, no interview

links.title: Spain Long-Term Visa from Bangladesh (BLS International, official)
Schengen Visa Refusal Rate 2024-2025 (HelloSafe barometer)

citizenship.summary: 10 years residence, NO dual citizenship for Bangladeshis (must renounce), plus DELE A2 + CCSE exams.
citizenship.reasoning: Standard naturalisation requires 10 years of legal residence for non-Ibero-American nationals, and Spain's dual-nationality treaties exclude Bangladesh - so Bangladeshis must formally renounce their original nationality at the citizenship oath, directly defeating this couple's dual-citizenship preference. Applicants must also pass the DELE A2 Spanish exam (Bangladeshis not exempt; only Ibero-Americans are) and the CCSE civics test. The long clock, forced renunciation and language bar are all decisive negatives, placing this near the bottom band; the strong passport once obtained is small consolation given the cost of entry.
evidence: 10 years legal residence for non-Ibero-American nationals (incl. Bangladeshis)
Dual-nationality treaties cover only Latin America, Andorra, Portugal, Philippines, Eq. Guinea - not Bangladesh
Renunciation of prior nationality required at the oath/ceremony
DELE A2 language exam required; Bangladeshis not exempt
CCSE civics/constitution test applies to virtually all applicants

links.title: Does Spain Allow Dual Citizenship? Rules & Requirements 2026 (MySpainVisa)
Spanish Citizenship by Residence: 2025 Guide (Lexidy)

post-study-work.summary: RD 1155/2024: 30h/week during studies + up to 24-month job-search permit; in-country switch to Blue Card, but permit itself doesn't authorise work.
post-study-work.reasoning: Under RD 1155/2024 (in force 20 May 2025) higher-education students may work up to 30h/week automatically, then take a job-search residence permit (sources cite 12 up to 24 months, non-renewable) to seek work or start a business. Conversion to a work permit / EU Blue Card is in-country and streamlined for these qualified engineers. Two caveats keep it below the strong band: the job-search permit does NOT itself authorise employment (a separate work permit is needed once a job is found), and the accompanying spouse cannot work during the study phase.
evidence: Higher-ed students may work up to 30h/week, built into the permit (RD 1155/2024)
Post-study job-search residence permit up to 12-24 months, non-extendable
Job-search permit does NOT authorise work; separate work permit needed on hire
In-country switch to EU Blue Card (no labour-market test, ~20 days)
Reform in force 20 May 2025; student permits valid for full course duration

links.title: Job Search Visa in Spain for students seeking employment (Immigration Spain)
Spain's New Residency Rules: RD 1155/2024 (360 Business Law)

spouse-family.summary: Student's spouse may accompany but is explicitly NOT authorised to work during studies - decisive for two earners; both work only in work phase.
spouse-family.reasoning: RD 1155/2024 lets a student's spouse/partner accompany (apply together or later if 90+ days remain), but official guidance states accompanying family members 'are not authorized to work' - and a student cannot sponsor family reunification under the general regime. For a two-engineer household this means one partner sits idle through the full MSc unless separately enrolled as a student (then 30h/week) or holding their own work permit. Both work freely once on work/Blue Card permits, lifting it above the worst band but the study-phase ban is a serious structural weakness.
evidence: Student's accompanying family members 'are not authorized to work' (RD 1155/2024)
Students cannot sponsor family reunification under the general regime
Family may apply together or later if 90+ days remain on student permit
Each partner enrolling separately as a student can independently work 30h/week
Both partners work freely once holding work/EU Blue Card permits

Spain's New Residency Rules: RD 1155/2024 (360 Business Law)

msc-study.summary: 200+ English-taught Master's, cheap public tuition (~EUR 2,700-6,600 non-EU/yr), 30h/week work; but accompanying spouse can't work.
msc-study.reasoning: Spain offers 200+ English-taught Master's and a public 60-ECTS MSc costs roughly EUR 2,700-6,600/year for non-EU students (fees set regionally; UPC Barcelona has cut non-EU master's fees), far cheaper than the UK/US. Students get an automatic 30h/week work right and the permit now covers the full study duration without annual renewals. It trails Germany's near-free tuition and limited-but-growing English provision, and the accompanying spouse cannot work during studies unless separately enrolled - both temper the score.
evidence: 200+ English-taught Master's programmes across Spanish universities
Public non-EU MSc ~EUR 2,700-6,600/year (60 ECTS); fees set per region
UPC Barcelona reduced non-EU master's tuition fees
Students may work up to 30h/week; permit valid for full course duration (RD 1155/2024)
Private universities EUR 10,000-30,000+/year for English Master's

links.title: Spain: Tuition fees & cost of living (Study.eu)
UPC reduces tuition fees for non-EU master's students (official)

pr-pathway.summary: Long-term residence at 5 years - but STUDENT years count only 50%, pushing real PR to ~7+ years from study start.
pr-pathway.reasoning: Long-term residence (larga duracion) is granted after 5 years of continuous legal residence with modest income (~EUR 600/month, 100% IPREM), valid health cover and clean record. The catch for a study-first pathway: student-visa years count only 50% toward the 5-year total, so a 2-year MSc contributes only ~1 year - realistic PR is ~7+ years from arrival. Work/Blue Card years count fully, the route is clear and renewable indefinitely, but the headline '5 years' is misleading for this couple.
evidence: Long-term residence after 5 years continuous legal residence
Student-visa years count only 50% toward permanent residence
Work-permit / Blue Card years count as full years toward the 5-year goal
Income bar low: ~EUR 600/month (100% IPREM, 2025)
Absences must not exceed ~10 months total in the 5-year period; TIE renews every 5 years

Long-Term Residency in Spain: 2025 Guide (RRYP Global)

income-cost.summary: Mid SW pay (Barcelona avg ~EUR 63.6k TC) vs record rents - Madrid rent ~37% of household income; two incomes save modestly.
income-cost.reasoning: Software engineers earn solid mid pay (Barcelona average TC ~EUR 63,597; Madrid range ~EUR 36k-75k; EUR 100k+ at senior international firms), and two incomes help, but Spain is in an acute housing squeeze that erodes savings. National rents hit a record ~EUR 15/m2/month in April 2026 (+5.2% YoY) and Madrid rent already absorbs ~37% of average household income with rental supply down 56% since the pandemic. Combined with high IRPF, net savings on two engineering incomes are decent but pressured - down from prior view given the worsening rent data.
evidence: Barcelona SW average total comp ~EUR 63,597; Madrid ~EUR 36k-75k (2026)
National average rent ~EUR 15/m2/month, record high, +5.2% YoY (April 2026)
Madrid rent ~37% of average household income (Dec 2025); big cities ~EUR 1,750/month
Rental supply down 56% since the pandemic (Idealista, 2025)
Cost of living below Germany/Nordics but housing now a major drag

healthcare.summary: Universal SNS, life expectancy ~84 (top in EU); students under 26 free, workers via social security, Convenio Especial ~EUR 60/month otherwise.
healthcare.reasoning: Spain's National Health System (SNS) is near-universal and high quality - life expectancy ~84, the best in the EU, ranked 26th of 110 on the 2024 Health Care Index. Students under 26 and all workers (via social-security contributions) are covered; others can pay into the Convenio Especial at ~EUR 60/month, though it does not auto-cover dependants. Public waiting-list strain pushes many to affordable private top-ups, holding it just below the top band; safety net (unemployment/parental benefits) is solid for contributors.
evidence: SNS near-universal; life expectancy ~84, highest in the EU
Spain ranked 26th of 110 on 2024 Health Care Index
Students under 26 access public healthcare free
Workers covered via social-security contributions
Convenio Especial ~EUR 60/month for under-65s; does not auto-cover dependants

links.title: The healthcare system in Spain (Expatica, 2026)

culture-language.summary: EF EPI 2025: 36th globally (540, 'moderate'); English works in tech hubs but Spanish needed for officialdom & citizenship (DELE A2 + CCSE).
culture-language.reasoning: Spain ranks only 36th on EF EPI 2025 (score 540, 'moderate', speaking just 462), so English usability for day-to-day officialdom is markedly weaker than northern Europe; tech workplaces in Madrid/Barcelona do run in English. Spanish is needed for bureaucracy and is a hard citizenship bar (DELE A2 + CCSE, no Bangladeshi exemption), and Catalonia adds Catalan in some official/educational settings. Livability (climate, food, social life) is excellent, but the language friction for this couple's long-term goals keeps it in the mixed band.
evidence: EF EPI 2025: Spain 36th globally, score 540 ('moderate'); speaking 462
English common in Barcelona/Madrid tech scene; weak for officialdom off-hub
Citizenship needs DELE A2 + CCSE; Bangladeshis not exempt
Catalan used in some official/educational contexts in Catalonia
High livability: climate, food, social life rated strongly

DELE A2 exam for Spanish citizenship 2025/2026 (Prep2Go)

safety-law.summary: WJP 2025: 25th of 143 (score 0.71, -0.6%); low violent crime, fair courts - but high petty theft in tourist cities.
safety-law.reasoning: Spain ranks 25th of 143 on the WJP Rule of Law Index 2025 with a score of 0.71 (down 0.6% YoY), upper-tier but below Germany and the Nordics. Violent crime is low and courts are broadly fair, but pickpocketing/petty theft is notably high in Barcelona/Madrid tourist zones, and 2025 saw anti-migrant unrest (Torre-Pacheco) that, while localised, dents the climate for visible minorities. Treatment of skilled migrants is generally fair with the usual extranjeria administrative slowness.
evidence: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: 25th of 143, score 0.71 (-0.6% YoY)
Low violent crime relative to global peers
High petty theft / pickpocketing in Barcelona & Madrid tourist areas
July 2025 anti-migrant unrest in Torre-Pacheco (Murcia)
Extranjeria administrative slowness a common friction

politics.summary: Pro-migration govt (RD 1155/2024; +46% work applications) but Sanchez minority fragile; Vox projected to ~double seats (33->60).
politics.reasoning: The PSOE-led government remains pro-skilled-migration - RD 1155/2024 expanded routes and applications to reside and work rose 46.2% to 724,000 (May-Oct 2025), with a 2026 plan to regularise ~500,000 migrants. But Sanchez leads a fragile, scandal-hit minority polling ~107 seats (far below 176), while Vox is projected to nearly double to ~60 seats, won ~17% in Extremadura (Dec 2025) and is re-entering PP regional coalitions on tougher immigration lines. Current policy is favourable but the trajectory is volatile and tilting against migrants, lowering the score.
evidence: RD 1155/2024 expanded routes; reside-and-work applications +46.2% to 724,000 (May-Oct 2025)
2026 plan to regularise ~500,000 undocumented migrants
PSOE polling ~107 seats vs 176 needed; corruption scandals weaken Sanchez
Vox projected to jump from 33 to ~60 seats; ~17% in Dec 2025 Extremadura vote
PP-Vox regional coalitions tightening immigration rules

Spain's far-right Vox returns to regional power with PP (Euronews, 2026)

tax.summary: High regular IRPF (19-47%); Beckham 24% flat rate needs no Spanish residence in prior 5 years - excludes study-first arrivals.
tax.reasoning: Regular IRPF is progressive 19-47% (47% above EUR 300,000; ~45% above EUR 60,000, region-dependent) plus employee social security - a high burden on SW salaries. The Beckham regime offers a flat 24% up to EUR 600,000 for six years, but it requires NO Spanish tax residence in the prior 5 years and relocation for a Spanish employer, so a couple who study in Spain first cannot use it when they switch to work. The realistic burden for this study-first pathway is the high regular rate.
evidence: IRPF progressive 19-47%; ~45% above EUR 60,000, 47% above EUR 300,000 (region-dependent)
Beckham flat 24% on income up to EUR 600,000 for 6 tax years
Beckham requires no Spanish tax residence in prior 5 years - excludes study-first arrivals
Beckham needs a Spanish employer (or Digital Nomad Visa for remote)
Employee social-security contributions on top of income tax

muslim-diaspora.summary: ~50k Bangladeshis, 1.085M Muslims (Jan 2025), Barcelona/Madrid hubs, easy halal & mosques - but 2025 anti-Muslim unrest rising.
muslim-diaspora.reasoning: Spain hosts ~50,000 Bangladeshis and ~1.085 million Muslims (Jan 2025), with established South Asian communities in Barcelona (264 mosques in the province) and Madrid's Lavapies, where halal butchers, restaurants and grocers are abundant - real settlement support for this couple. But the sentiment trend is deteriorating: the July 2025 Torre-Pacheco anti-migrant violence, a mosque arson near Barcelona, and Vox proposals to ban the hijab in public spaces are material negatives, pulling the score down from the prior view despite the community's size and Andalusian heritage.
evidence: ~50,000 Bangladeshis in Spain; ~1.085M Muslims as of January 2025
Barcelona province has 264 mosques/Muslim places of worship
Madrid's Lavapies: Bangladeshi/Pakistani halal restaurants, butchers, grocers
July 2025 Torre-Pacheco anti-migrant violence; mosque arson near Barcelona
Vox proposed banning the hijab in all public spaces (2025)
